issue_title: build dictionary, goals reference could set a css style for oss-pants vs. organization-internal-weirdness
issue_body:
Twitter's pants has a plugin that registers some things. We're thinking about using 'goal builddict' to generate an internal equivalent of the BUILD Dictionary and the Goals Reference. 

It'd be nice if there was some way to style real-pants things vs twitter-pants things. E.g., if we want the doc to show only twitter-internal-quirks, we could set the real-pants style to { display: none } .

One way to implement this: give each item a css style based on the name of the module that implements it. So to style real-pants things, we look for the style named implemented-by-pants. And for twitter-pants things, twitter would set a style for implemented-by-whatever-module-these-come-from.

(so far, this is more of a could-be-a-cool-idea than a we-must-do-this item, but jotting it down here)

issue_title: Confluence publisher breaks on Mac OS 10.12.5
issue_body:
Pants 1.3.0rc3+1334050808 is failing with the following exception message when I am trying to publish a page to Confluence:
```
$ ./pants confluence ...

15:48:13 00:00 [main]
               (To run a reporting server: ./pants server)
15:48:13 00:00   [setup]
15:48:13 00:00     [parse]
               Executing tasks in goals: markdown -> confluence
15:48:13 00:00   [markdown]
15:48:13 00:00     [markdown]
                   Emitted ....
                     Processed ... to ....
                     Processed ... to ...
15:48:14 00:01   [confluence]
15:48:14 00:01     [confluence]Please enter confluence password for ...:

15:48:21 00:08   [complete]
               FAILURE
Exception caught: (<class 'ssl.SSLError'>)

Exception message: [SSL: SSLV3_ALERT_HANDSHAKE_FAILURE] sslv3 alert handshake failure (_ssl.c:590)
```
The associated Pants target:
```
page(name = '...',
  source = '...',
  provides =[
     wiki_artifact(
        wiki = confluence,
        space = '...',
        title = '...',
        parent = '...'
     )
   ]
 )
```

Pants uses 4 different directories at the root of the project: `.cache`, `dist`, `.pants.d` and `.pids`. This feels untidy and increases the likelihood of conflicts with other tools/existing code. 
It seems that a convention for work directories is `.NAME`, such as `.git` or `.idea`, so I would suggest the following re-organisation:
```
<project-home>
   + .git
   + .pants
      + dist
      + pids
      + cache
      + ... (everything that's currently in .pants.d)
```

I understand this might break things but it can be taken as an opportunity to fix hardcoded paths such as:
- https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/261
- https://github.com/pantsbuild/intellij-pants-plugin/issues/167

**Note** : after discussing this on slack a couple of reasons came up as to why things are organised as they are now:

1. _`dist` lives outside of `.pants.d` so that it doesn't get wiped out by `clean-all`_
 
 IMO that's a rather surprising behavior as `clean-all` is described as a mean to clean your workspace. It's also unexpected for people coming from other build tools.

2. _`.pants.d` is a black box_
 
 I guess that's just a matter of defining what `.pants.d` is. Very much like `target` with Maven or SBT, `.pants` can be defined as the root to everything that is produced by pants. And if things within `.pants` must be flagged as internal or unstable, then it's always possible to move then under something like `.pants/internal`. IMO that's unnecessary.

issue_title: protobuf_gen.py uses the project base for proto file imports
issue_body:
In https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/codegen/protobuf/java/protobuf_gen.py around line 39 the protobuf-java compiler is being passed the base of the project as it's source root. This means that within protobuf files you need to organize your imports like `import "root/path/to/file/proto_file.proto`

This is suboptimal because it's not actually how proto files are idiomatically linked. If I have 2 files that live at `"path/to/file/a.proto"` and `"path/to/file/b.proto"` a.proto can normally import b.proto by just saying `import "b.proto"`. Instead, for pants to compile a java_protobuf_library target you need to call `import "path/to/file/b.proto"` , but then IDEs like IntelliJ can't link the files properly and you have a good code red problem.

Scalapb-pants doesn't have this problem - it expects files to be linked idiomatically. When trying to use both in a project as part of upgrading protobuf-java to scalapb this problem became quite clear. See https://github.com/scalapb/scalapb-pants/blob/master/src/python/trueaccord/pants/scalapb/tasks/scalapb_gen.py

We are working to provide more in-depth support of C and C++ code, e.g. by providing the compiler and linker (#5490) and hooking it up to other languages (#5815). At some point, this will eclipse the contents of `contrib/cpp`. When that happens, we should deprecate that module.

issue_title: Too many decimal places in output
issue_body:
Some bibliography so it doesn't sound like pure opinion:

![how_many_decimal_places](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8271090/10252703/ab91fd9c-690f-11e5-9988-625808be2be6.png)
[from Measuring the User Experience: Collecting, Analyzing, and Presenting Usability Metrics]

Also, Mathematica, Octave, MATLAB and other mathematical software hide some decimal places when it doesn't matter.

I get some lines like the following:

    request_processing_seconds_sum 9.3512252295
    request_latency_seconds_sum 15.77115141185

And I am suggesting that they get changed to:

    request_processing_seconds_sum 9.351
    request_latency_seconds_sum 15.771

How many decimal places we are going to use is open for discussion, but 10 seems **not** an OK decision for a final product.

---

There is also a need to define where we are going to perform the rounding (we could truncate, but rounding seems statistically fairer). It could be done in the ``_samples`` functions of the classes defined in core.py, but maybe there is a better place.

Thanks for reading.

In our tests, API returns encode/decode errors for the ```unicode``` objects when the object contains non ascii chars like 'ÖöŞşÇçİıĞğÜü', however it works fine with ```unicode``` objects when chars are in ascii range. 

All of the test files here on github uses typed ```str``` objects, so they pass. However if you make an API call using a ```unicode``` object, for example if you change the address as such: ```u'Nidakule Göztepe, Merdivenköy Mah. Bora Sok. No:1'``` it fails while building the pki string (because there are direct string casts like ```str(value) ```). It only fails when the object contains none ascii chars. 

We tried converting ```unicode``` objects to ```str``` before passing them to API (encoding utf-8), but we still get encode/decode errors because of the multiple casts.

In any case, nearly all of the big Python frameworks (like Django) rely on unicode and for a good reason. I believe it would be a much better practice if the API would work with unicode objects without a problem. 

Please let us know If you think this error is on our part, if not, can you please add coverage for unicode objects. This problem keeps us stuck. 

Looking forward to your guidance. 
Thank you. 

--------
To reproduce the problem
- Use Python 2.7
- Convert one of the address lines in your tests to unicode (as they contain none ascii Turkish chars) like so:

> `address = dict([('address', u'Nidakule Göztepe, Merdivenköy Mah. Bora Sok. No:1')])`

- Additionally you can convert other strings to unicode as well, to see that it works if the string doesn't contains none-ascii char.

issue_title: 'Database is Locked' error when channel importing is running
issue_body:
We get errors in `jonboiser`'s `available-channels` branch. It's not reliably replicating (!), but the general observations are:

- It happens during `startremotechannelimport` or `diskchannelimport`
- The actual error is raised when another API or session call happens

My sense here is that it's caused by the long content import and annotation phase of channel importing.

Possible solutions:
- shorten length of transactions

issue_title: Does not work on OSX Darwin
issue_body:
The use of TCPKeepAliveAdapter from requests-toolbelt means that the marathon client doesn't work on Mac OS X.  It is fixed in the upstream in the commit below, but is not release by the requests team.

https://github.com/requests/toolbelt/commit/ec4fb7c783c2df09d84dae3fc794cf5a4f8b02bf

This is just an informational notice to other users.  Until requests releases the above fix, marathon-python doesn't work on Darwin without providing a self constructed connection to marathon SSE.

Amazon document indicates that current version is 2013-08-01 and best practices document advice to use latest version.

"The Product Advertising API uses the default API version 2013-08-01 unless you supply a different value for the Version parameter. To use a different version, you must include it in the Version parameter in your requests."
[Amazon Advertising API Best Practices] (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSECommerceService/latest/DG/CHAP_BestProgrammingPractices.html)

issue_title: Document / Throw Warnings for Non-implemented JTS Constraints
issue_body:
JSON Table Schema defines several [constraints](http://dataprotocols.org/json-table-schema/#field-constraints) (`minLength`, `maxLength`, `pattern`, `minimum`, `maximum`, and `oneOf`) that are not yet implemented by goodtables. When checking a delimited file against a JTS file with column constraints, goodtables does not throw any warnings that some constraints are unchecked or ignored. Instead, the constraints are implicitly ignored and goodtables returns the "Data is Valid" message. This is a form of silent failure that is misleading to the user; they don't know that their data isn't checked against these constraints.

Even though this software is still being developed, unimplemented constraints should be documented and if unimplemented constraints are used in the JTS file, goodreads should output a warning that constraints are being ignored.

# Overview

Per specs implementation chapter there is missed ability to updated `Schema.descriptor` - http://specs.frictionlessdata.io/implementation/

# Tasks

- [ ] implement updating ability for `Schema.descriptor`
- [ ] sync this solution with other stack libraries

As an acos_client user, I'd like the created or updated virtual server to be returned from `client.slb.virtual_server.create` and `client.slb.virtual_server.update`

 - [ ] v2.1

issue_title: Unable to install conda package from root of drive
issue_body:
On Windows it's not possible to install a local package (from .tar.bz2) if the package is located in the root of a drive. This is probably an error related to how the package name is separated from the folder on Windows. See full traceback below. You can see it's trying to create a folder beginning with ":".

issue_title: activate and deactivate script paths on Windows
issue_body:
When changing environments the activate and deactivate scripts generated on Windows do not include the path in quotes, so you have things like

    #!/usr/bin/env bash 
    source C:\Users\My Name\Anaconda2\Scripts\activate "$@"

and neither command works. It works OK using the .bat files, but not used "source activate" etc when used from e.g. MingW.

issue_title: Cloning root env creates broken environment
issue_body:
Not sure if I'm doing anything wrong, but the following sequence of steps causes an error:

1. Create env by cloning root
2. Try to update said env.

Here's my session, starting from a newly installed miniconda with some of the "typical scipy stack" packages already installed into it (but not a full anaconda):

```
denali[~]> conda create -n stable --clone root
Source:      /Users/fperez/usr/conda

issue_title: ctable and cols setitem allows you to set value to ANY type with the right __len__
issue_body:
```
test_ctable = bcolz.ctable(numpy.zeros(2, dtype='f8, i4'))
test_ctable['f0'] = dict(a=1,b='bar')  # ('a',2), ['foo',tuple], etc all work, just has to have len() == 2
```
``dict`` is missing all kinds of functions that are later expected (like say purge and flush if I delete ``f0``).

This goes through ``__setitem__`` for ``ctable`` and ``cols``. The former passes straight to the latter b/c I'm setting an existing name. The latter setitem only checks len:
https://github.com/Blosc/bcolz/blob/master/bcolz/ctable.py#L86

Context:
I ran across this while trying to see if there's a way to do ``+=`` on bcolz objects. In particular:
```
test_ctable = bcolz.ctable(numpy.zeros(2, dtype='f8, i4'))
test_ctable['f0'] += 1.0  # fails, no += operator
test_ctable['f0'] += numpy.ones(2)  # success! b/c numpy's ladd and radd are both type-promoting and setitem accepts anything w/length 2
type(test_ctable)  # numpy.ndarray
test_ctable['f0'] += 1  # success! b/c it's a numpy array now
```

issue_title: "SystemError" Error returned without exception set on cigar strings with soft clipping without query sequence present
issue_body:
Hello !

I've noticed a possible issue with psyam with BAM file. I noticed for alignments that did not have query sequences stored (e.g., "*", according to the BAM standard, query_sequence is not required) that ```query_alignment_end``` (```qend```) and ```query_alignment_length``` (```qlen```) return an exception: 

```SystemError: error return without exception set```

It seems that soft clipping of 1 (e.g., 124=1S) with an alignment without a query sequence will cause this exception. Changing the cigar string to have soft clipping of 2 (e.g., 123=2S), results in -2 being returned for ```qend```.

In https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/master/pysam/calignedsegment.pyx:

The function definition specifies an exception is raised when -1 is returned. 

```cdef inline int32_t getQueryEnd(bam1_t *src) except -1:```

I think what is happening here is that since the query sequence is not present, the length = 0.
```cdef uint32_t end_offset = src.core.l_qseq```

Then later when end offset is modified for soft clipping:
```elif op == BAM_CSOFT_CLIP:
                end_offset -= cigar_p[k] >> BAM_CIGAR_SHIFT```

end_offset is being set to -1, returning the exception. The result of -2 for ```qend``` when soft clipping is 2, seems to confirm this.

The behavior I expected was for pysam to calculate the ```qend``` using the cigar string when the query sequence is not present.

issue_title: Azure CLI Feature: Make aliases for common commands like "create"
issue_body:
### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here: pip

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here: azure-cli (2.0.3)

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: macOS 10.12.3

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: bash on macOS

---

### Description

We often need to `create` a resource using the CLI. Aliases are super common with many CLI's. Some of our Azure CLI flags have aliases, but our commands should benefit from this too. Consider how often we create something ... we currently type:

`az group create -n PapaResourceGroup -l "East US"`

but we could type

`az group c -n PapaResourceGroup -l "East US"`

Now consider 3 commands in a row ...

```
az group create -n PapaResourceGroup -l eastus

az storage account create -n papastorage -g PapaResourceGroup --sku Standard_LRS -l eastus

az functionapp create -n papafunction -g PapaResourceGroup -s papastorage -c eastus
```

now see this ...

```
az group c -n PapaResourceGroup -l eastus

az storage account c -n papastorage -g PapaResourceGroup --sku Standard_LRS -l eastus

az functionapp c -n papafunction -g PapaResourceGroup -s papastorage -c eastus
```

I also think it would be great to have aliases for other commands, but the quick win would be "create" since we use it in so many places.

cc // @SyntaxC4

Failed test
```
python -m unittest azure.cli.core.tests.test_help.HelpTest.test_help_long_description_from_docstring
```

Issues:
1) The test is not run in CI.
2) The mocking of the test loader seems to be difficult.

issue_body:
The ComputeManagementClient build config with a parameter 'long_running_operation_retry_timeout' but all the operations are looking for 'long_running_operation_timeout'. 

Config definition:  (https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure-mgmt-compute/azure/mgmt/compute/compute_management_client.py#L56)

Example (mis)usage: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/azure-mgmt-compute/azure/mgmt/compute/operations/virtual_machines_operations.py#L126-L131

Per announcement https://blogs.msdn.microsoft.com/azuregov/2018/04/03/azure-government-aad-authority-endpoint-update/ , the azure government AAD endpoint is changed to https://login.microsoftonline.us . However, it is still being used here https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_cloud.py#L173 . I was able to work-around the issue by passing custom `auth_uri` and `token_uri`

```
ServicePrincipalCredentials(
                        cloud_environment=msrestazure.azure_cloud.AZURE_US_GOV_CLOUD,
                        client_id=clientId,
                        secret=clientSecret,
                        tenant=tenant,
                        token_uri = "https://login.microsoftonline.us/{}/oauth2/token".format(tenant),
                        auth_uri="https://login.microsoftonline.us/{}/oauth2/authorize".format(tenant)
                    )
```

`openaddr-process-one` creates a file named `output.txt` which has logfile output. when the program finishes that file is mode 0600. We want 0644. The permission is probably being set when it's created as a tempfile.

Now websocket writer has custom flow control.
It's maybe not bad but overcomplicates things.
Since #2836 is done websocket writer uses coroutines everywhere, the transition should be smooth.

No Public API changes but `limit=DEFAUTLT_LIMIT` and `MSG_SIZE` will be dropped.
I see related micro-optimizations made by @fafhrd91 and @pfreixes but I believe the performance impact will be negligible.

Proposal from #1547: go back to using require.js for this notebook, and leave webpack for newer projects, such as jupyterlab.

The switch to webpack in the legacy notebook seems to make lots of backward-compatibility maintenance impossible, and has been the source of numerous issues (#1547, #1439, #1431, etc.). Following some discussion in #1547, I think it's not feasible to maintain our goals of backward-compatibility in the existing notebook javascript while adopting webpack. The key point being that it seems to be impossible to include a module in the webpack, and then load it later with require, which is a necessity for numerous extensions and official APIs.

If that's not true, and we are just failing to configure things correctly, there might be a nicer, simpler fix. But this gist is, for the notebook js the following two things must return the same object:

- `require('notebook/js/cell')` in our packed-up sources
- `require('notebook/js/cell')` at runtime, in outputs, custom.js, or nbextensions

(and so on, for codemirror and everything else we load via require). The behavior in master is to load a different copy of the module, which is causing all kinds of things to break.

issue_title: Error with `w.T.dot(x)`, correct if `np.dot(w, x)` is used
issue_body:
Why does Method 2 below (using `np.dot`) return the correct gradient, while Method 1 returns an array of zeros?  Perhaps an exception should be thrown if the parser detects dot products of the form `w.T.dot(x)` instead of `np.dot(w, x)` if the latter format is really necessary.

```python
import numpy as np
from autograd import grad
w = np.arange(5).astype('float')

# Method 1
f = lambda x: w.T.dot(x)
df = grad(f)
df(np.random.randn(5))
# >>> array([0., 0., 0., 0., 0.])

# Method 2
f = lambda x: np.dot(w, x)
df = grad(f)
df(np.random.randn(5))
# >>> array([0., 1., 2., 3., 4.])

```

issue_title: Feature request: smarter primitive for container_types
issue_body:
I've been noticing that `grad()` can be quite memory intensive, due to the tape.

As a workaround, I've been making some of my subroutines `@primitive`, and assigning their derivatives thru `defgrad`. This way, the gradient for the subroutine gets computed "off" the tape.

One issue is if the primitive subroutine takes a dict containing nodes, i.e. `subroutine(a_dict)`. Then `primitive.__call__()` doesn't know to call `subroutine.gradmaker` because `not isinstance(a_dict, Node)`. The result is the computations in `subroutine` continue to be added to the tape.

My solution is to convert `a_dict` into a `DictNode`; then the subroutine truly acts as a primitive.

It would be nice if `primitive.__call__()` did this automatically (i.e., not just check if the arguments are Nodes, but if they are container types with Nodes inside). Otherwise, primitives that take container_types aren't really primitive.

If a collection is defined for the outputs of a tool:

```
<outputs>
    <collection name="intermediate_results" label="Intermediate results" type="list">
        <data name="some_name_tag" format="fastq" label="Fancy_output" from_work_dir="some_name_tag.fastq" />
        <data name="some_name_tag_2" format="fastq" label="Fancy_output_2" from_work_dir="some_name_tag_2.fastq" />
    </collection>
</outputs>
```

and the testing is written wrong in the following way:

```
<tests>
    <test>
        <output name="some_name_tag" file="some_file" lines_diff="2"/>
        <output name="some_name_tag_2" file="some_file_2" lines_diff="2"/>
    </test>
</tests>
```

instead of:

```
<tests>
    <test>
         <output_collection name="intermediate_results">
            <element name="some_name_tag" file="some_file" lines_diff="2"/>
            <element name="some_name_tag_2" file="some_file" lines_diff="2"/>
        </output_collection>
    </test>
</tests>
```

than it happens that `planemo lint` says every thing is correct which is obviously wrong. The `planemo test` command executes it and fails. But the reason for the fail is now weird: Instead of crashing because some file could not be found, it fails because the name of the output files and the content of files are somehow random assigned to each other. And than the testcases fail. In the above example it would mean that the correct computed 'some_name_tag'-file would maybe have the content of 'some_name_tag_2'-file. 

It would be nice if the linter could see that the test case is defined wrong, or that the testing looks for the collection and if it is not there it should raise an error instead of reassigning file names and content randomly without any warning. 

I hope I explained my issue in a way you can reproduce. If not I have a concrete implementation of this in my account and I could explain what you have to do if requested.

What happens 5 years from now when your dependencies get deleted off the internet? Well, if you have a local copy you can use an `override`, but that means you're probably not syncing the right version. You can rewrite your project's history, so that the new URL is there from the beginning, but now you've screwed all your branches and (PGP signed?!) tags. We need a way to build an old `peru.yaml` file on a future internet without throwing things away.

This is complicated slightly by the fact that critical-looking fields like `url` are actually implemented by *plugins*. Peru doesn't know anything about them. We could hardcode something that treats the `url` field specially, but then angels would shed bitter tears.

I think what we need is a generic way to modify module fields, without actually editing `peru.yaml`. We actually talked about this a little bit in https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/issues/75. I'm imagining a file like this:

```yaml
url:
    "https://github.com/foo/bar": "https://githubwaybackmachine.com/foo/bar"
```

We could consider doing something clever with regular expressions (like "all github.com URLs should become bitbucket.com URLs"), but I don't know if that will be really necessary. We'll probably want a default location for this file, something like `overrides.yaml` in the same directory as `peru.yaml`. We'll want a flag to allow setting the file explicitly, and in this case I think we'll also want an environment variable (similar to `PERU_CACHE_DIR`), because you might need to set this globally. In fact, I think in this case we're going to want *cumulative* overrides, so that you can set these globally and project-specifically at the same time.

But the name "overrides" sucks, because I don't want to overload it with the `overrides` command, which is probably always going to store its state in a project-specific place (because it uses module names, which aren't expected to be unique across projects). I need to come up with something better...

We might want to print some kind of warning when you run a build that has fields remapped, so you don't set something globally and then forget you've done it and get confused. In fact, we should probably go back and do that for the `override` command too.

Using Sonnet is practice right now is really much harder than it should be:
Because no pre-built binaries are available and Sonnet isn't published on PyPI, every user has to go through the difficult process of installing bazel and building Sonnet themselves, as exemplified by the many issues filed by people having problems with the build process.
I have had to jump through many hoops to find a way to use Sonnet in a project which I train on Google Cloud ML Engine (the solution was to build Sonnet inside a Debian Docker container on my Mac and then to upload the resulting wheel to Google Cloud ML). This would have been much easier if binary builds were available for download.
Secondly there is no versioning yet, so it's extremely difficult to ensure different machines or environments actually run the same Sonnet version or to tell which commits break compatibility with previous versions.
I understand that Sonnet is an early piece of software, but given how widely you publicized the open-source release, could you comment on your plans to implement some versioning and packaging to make it more practical to use?

It's becoming pretty common for queries to return large results sets that take a long time to return. We might want to change `db.__call__` to return a result proxy that needs to be fetched like `db(**query).fetch()`. In early days we were reluctant to do that in the original pass because we were getting pushback on the verbosity of the API, but ultimately I think that was the wrong call. Returning a result proxy would make things fast and also allow us to incorporate things like `limit` which are hard to fit into our elided query API.

I think we can leave `db.__getitem__` as it is because those query results sets scale with the size of the input argument. Since that's the API that people use the most, this might not even break very much user code. Of course it will break some.

issue_title: Incorrectly identify queryset as not iterable when using exclude.
issue_body:
[pip_freeze.txt](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint-django/files/2189146/pip_freeze.txt)

```python
return [periodique.pk for periodique in Periodique.objects.exclude(periode__isnull=True)]
```
with this code, pylint detects the following error : 
pylint: not-an-iterable / Non-iterable value Periodique.objects.exclude(periode__isnull=True) is used in an iterating context (col 48)

If I replace exclude by filter, no error is detected by pylint
I would like to see support of Cisco Spark event API endpoint to be added.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12187260/33522193-0722cbd2-d821-11e7-9528-a8c845710b7f.png)

issue_title: ASP.NET Core Web Application Visual Basic (VB)
issue_body: Any idea on when we will be able to create new projects for ASP.NET Core Web Application using Visual Basic?

# General

When .Net Core 2.0 was released, it seems to include a non-xbuild compatible .Targets file.

We've been debugging this issue here https://github.com/fsprojects/Paket/issues/2616 – and it's indeed the targets-file of .Net Core that is the problem.

Could you please fix your package an unbreak me?

issue_title: Calling sudo from inside a script = bad
issue_body:
So typically people should be aware root actions are about to be performed when they type in a command.

$ letsencrypt --help would not instictually be thought of as a command needing root access.  It's fine that it does but not ideal.  What is troubling is the use of sudo inside the script.

This means that if they had just run a few commands as root (someone working on their https would be in server configuration mode) and then decided to read the most available cli documentation on your command.  BOOM.  Let's install all the things.

A better solution would be to reject running without root and give a prompt stating that root access is need, then allow the user to explicitly grant it for your program.

The golden rule should be never include sudo in anything.  I can't think of any tool on debian that does.  I know on arch systemctl prompts for raised privilage but it doesn't use sudo because each issue requires granted privaliges.  Let the user manage root access.  Don't seripticiously gain root access through the back door without permission.

I'm sure you figured they would be prompted so it's all the same but sudo does have a grace period.  It is equal level of convinience for the user to sign for it on the outside of the program and is just polite.

Anyways hopefully this was just a mistake ;)

issue_title: can you propagate_activations() from just one bank?

issue_body:
One pretty common pain point is that the JSON spec can't serialize or deserialize datetimes. I suggest that we can add a `dateutil.json` submodule that would provide a common JSON serializer/deserializer plugin for the `json` module.

The main issues with this are:

1. How to specify how datetimes are stored - there are a few obvious ways to do this and it really depends on your use case. How can we add some customizability without ruining the convenience of such a thing?
2. This is semi-blocked on #76, and in general we need a consistent mechanism for serializing time zones to do it the "right way".
3. We should see if any sort of standards have already evolved on this front. If they haven't it's not a big deal, but we don't want to be re-inventing the wheel here.

issue_title: Integrate DLEstimator with Spark ML ImageSchema
issue_body:
As of a few months ago, it looks like now Spark has [native support](https://blogs.technet.microsoft.com/machinelearning/2018/03/05/image-data-support-in-apache-spark/) for reading directories of images into a Spark Dataframe. Specifically, the `pyspark.ml.image.ImageSchema.readImages(path_to_img_folder)` function returns a DataFrame that has the schema:
```
root
 |-- image: struct (nullable = true)
 |    |-- origin: string (nullable = true)
 |    |-- height: integer (nullable = false)
 |    |-- width: integer (nullable = false)
 |    |-- nChannels: integer (nullable = false)
 |    |-- mode: integer (nullable = false)
 |    |-- data: binary (nullable = false)
```

Now that this image DataFrame functionality exists, has anyone tried to create a Spark ML Pipeline that starts with `ImageSchema.readImages()`, converts the images to `Arrays`, and then uses a BigDL model to perform image featurization using a pre-trained `DLModel`? 

My goal is to implement a Spark Pipeline like: `Image URIs --> ImageSchema.readImages(), get a DataFrame with binary image data --> Reshape image data to Multi-dimensional arrays --> Transform DataFrame using pre-trained DLModel (e.g. ResNet50 featurizer) --> Ouptut n-dim Feature Vectors`?

This [house similarities](https://software.intel.com/en-us/articles/using-bigdl-to-build-image-similarity-based-house-recommendations) article is the closest snippet I could find to a code sample where a `DLClassifier` is applied to a DataFrame with image data (in the form of `Arrays`), but I haven't yet found any way to integrate this with Spark's new `ImageSchema` functionality.

Anyone have recommendations on what the best way to proceed with such an image featurization pipeline would be? I'd like to keep as much computation within the same Spark ML pipeline as possible.

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: String contains substring validity times out
issue_body:
Hello, 

I'm adding strings to Rosette. I currently am working on some simplifications. One simplification that I make is that a string should always contain all of its substrings. 

I'm testing my simplifications against Z3 to make sure we don't disagree on anything. This simplification works in Z3 with a bounded length, but currently times out for me (I wait a few minutes) when I try with an unbounded length. I understand validity in some fragments of the theory of strings is undecidable with unbounded lengths, but I think this case is simple enough that it might be worth considering separately. 

If it isn't, or if you try not to special-case too many things, then feel free to ignore this.

Here's the query: 

```
(set-option :model_validate true)

(declare-fun c0 () String)
(declare-fun c1 () Int)
(declare-fun c2 () Int)
(assert (not (str.contains c0 (str.substr c0 c1 (- c2 c1)))))
(assert (and (<= 0 c1) (<= c1 c2) (<= c2 (str.len c0))))
(check-sat)

(get-model)
```

    return [self.info_from_entry(entry) for entry in self.get_entries()]
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/netdisco/discoverables/yamaha.py", line 27, in info_from_entry
    service['X_unitDescURL'][1:])
KeyError: 'X_unitDescURL'
```

The R-N301 is an early Yamaha network player that doesn't quite follow the same protocol as the later musiccast players. It does haven an equivalent to `X_unitDescURL`, but replacing this in just causes the downstream library to crash, and as this is a discontinued player I'm not sure fixing that is the right way to go.

At the simplest level I was thinking just replacing this into https://github.com/home-assistant/netdisco/blob/master/netdisco/discoverables/yamaha.py#L31:

```
    def get_entries(self):
        """Get all the Yamaha uPnP entries."""
        devices = self.find_by_device_description({
            "manufacturer": "Yamaha Corporation",
            "deviceType": "urn:schemas-upnp-org:device:MediaRenderer:1"
        })
        return [device for device in devices if
                device.description['device']['modelNumber'] not in
                INCOMPATIBLE_MODELS]
```

with a static list of incompatible model numbers, but I'm not sure if there's a better way.

issue_body:
| Camera Model                       | D435 |

| SDK Version                            |  2.13 | 
| Language                            | LabVIEW 2017 32-bit  | 
| Segment			|  Robotics   | 

### Issue Description
Feature Request

First off, thanks for providing us with LabVIEW wrappers. That saved 90% of the time needed to get started just by seeing the examples and having all the library calls prototyped out. 

However, when I downloaded the latest wrappers, the DLL calls seem to be set to an absolute path: C:\Users\agrunnet\Documents\libRealSense6\realsense2.dll

Going forward though, I'm going to be moving these wrappers to Ubuntu 16.04 to run on an UP Board, our on-board vision processing computer. Using the current implementation, this would require us to update all 80ish VIs to use the new realsense2.so path for Linux. 

Rather than going through an updating every function's Call Library Function to point to a new absolute path, I selected "Specify path on diagram" and created a VI that gets the path to the file for me, and even detects if it's running in LabVIEW 32-bit or 64-bit. On Ubuntu/Linux, I'll just need to update this one VI's file path to point to the realsense2.so file rather than updating all 80ish VIs. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1508222/41869621-2ff65f48-787f-11e8-99a1-b8f7f029b59f.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1508222/41869727-7f72c4f8-787f-11e8-932e-ce838ab72aec.png)

I can create a Pull Request for this issue since I have updated all of the VIs to use this new "rs3 get dll path" call.

It seems like setting `'Required'` on a `ReferenceColumn` causes `db.sync()` to fail. Setting `'Required'` on, say, `StringColumn` works just fine.

Here's a small reproducible case:

```import orb


class MyReferencedTable(orb.Table):

    id = orb.IdColumn()


class MyTable(orb.Table):

    id = orb.IdColumn()
    name = orb.StringColumn(flags={'Required'})  # This works fine.
    reference = orb.ReferenceColumn(reference='MyReferencedTable',
                                    flags={'Required'})  # This doesn't.


def main():
    db = orb.Database('SQLite', 'test_required.db')
    db.activate()
    db.sync()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()```

It makes sense to me that the error would be thrown -- after all, we've defined a `NOT NULL` column without providing a default value. So, my question is why does it work with a `StringColumn` even though a default is not provided there. Thanks a bunch!

issue_title: Pymouse.screen_size() and Pymouse.position() cause Python launcher to appear os macOS
issue_body:
On macOS Sierra  
Latest pip3(9.0.1)
Latest PyUserInput according to `pip3 list --outdated`  
Latest Python3 according to brew(3.6.0)  

How to reproduce:  
on macOS Sierra, open terminal, type `python3`(brew installed)  
type `from pymouse import PyMouse`  
type `m = PyMouse()`  
type `m.position()`  
Python launcher will pop up in dock  

Expected behavior: Python launcher shouldn't pop up in the dock  

This only happens with these two methods, all other methods are find. No other problems are found with the methods. (that I know of)

Current OT only support multiple milliseconds timer, and a single microseconds timer used by [CSMA  backoff](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/mac/mac.cpp#L109) (only enabled by nRF52840 now).

To support the coming new features, we need multiple microseconds timer, the change proposal:

1. Platform alarm APIs update

    - Combine current two alarm APIs ([alarm](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/alarm.h), [usec-alarm](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/usec-alarm.h)) to single microseconds alarm API, platforms which could not support microseconds can still provide milliseconds accuracy but with the same microseconds alarm APIs.

2. `class Timer` update
    Option 1:
    - Replace current `uint32_t  mFireTime` field with the structure [otPlatUsecAlarmTime](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/include/openthread/platform/usec-alarm.h#L61)
    - This option looks much concise, although it will increase 2 bytes RAM space for each `Timer`. 

    Option 2:
    - Create a new `class TimerUsec` based on `class Timer`, with an extra microseconds field.
    - This option will complicate the `class TimerScheduler` to handle the two timers (two queues, time comparison between them), it will increase some code size.

I prefer option 1 more, please let me know your preference and other better options.

issue_title: Missing jitters for responses to MLE Parent Request/Link Request
issue_body:
Both Thread Specifications and Certification Test Plan define that responses for MLE Parent Requests must be delayed by a random jitter (up to 500 ms for Router only request, and up to 1000 ms for the second request). I'm not sure if this is currenty covered by test plan, it may be in the final version. The same applies for responses to multicast MLE Link Requests (up to 1000 ms).

Also I have a question regarding Mle::BecomeChild function. Why is the initial MLE Parent Request delayed by 1000 ms? Is this a overlook, or are there any implementation-specific reasons for that?

    def Umlaut_toASCII(self, s):

issue_title: letsencrypt-auto ui/ux:  perceptible feedback to show authentication method has been selected
issue_body:
#### Observed Behavior:  Recipe:
* Follow the letsencrypt-auto procedure to the point where it puts up a curses-based menu asking "How would you like to authenticate".
* Select an answer, and then OK.
* Observe that almost nothing changes.  The question and the OK button remain on the screen.  To all appearances, nothing has happened, and the question still needs answering.  A super-astute observer might notice that the cursor has moved to the lower-left corner of the screen, but on my screen this is essentially imperceptible.
* Many seconds later some visible progress occurs.

#### Desired and Expected Behavior: Suggestions:
1.  Subtraction part: As soon as the OK button is pressed, the menu should go away.
2.  Addition part:  There should be a clear statement that the process has moved to the next step.  For example, if the authentication process is about to begin, it might be appropriate to emit a line of text saying:  
``Authenticating (this may take a few seconds) ...``
3. If we want to get fancy and give the user some feedback about what he just selected, that could be made explicit:  
``Authenticating via apache2  (this may take a few seconds) ...``

Given how verbose other parts of the process are, this tiny bit of feedback would not be excessive.

#### Also:
The same ideas should apply to whatever other menus there are.  User actions should provide immediate perceptible feedback.

issue_title: Mention line in (Apache) conf file in case of Augeas parse/syntax error
issue_body:
See also the community thread [Certbot renew fails on current fedora 24 w/ apache](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/certbot-renew-fails-on-current-fedora-24-w-apache/25046).

In summary:

When a syntax/parse error occurs when Augeas tries to parse Apache configuration files, `certbot` raises an error and mentions the file in question and the Augeaus error message.

Unfortunately, the *message* itself doesn't contain a line number, so the user has to spit through all the lines, perhaps not even knowing why Augeas complains in the first place. It would be very helpful if the error message also would contain a line number.

On [some blog post](https://stomp.colorado.edu/blog/blog/2011/01/07/on-finding-and-fixing-augeas-parse-errors/) I found Augeas does provide a line number, but this is separate from the message. And `certbot` [only fetches the message, not the line number](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/master/certbot-apache/certbot_apache/augeas_configurator.py#L73).

Could we, for example, change the code to something like:

                msg = (
                    "There has been an error in parsing the file %s on line %s: %s",
                    # Strip off /augeas/files and /error
                    path[13:len(path) - 6], self.aug.get(path + "/line"), self.aug.get(path + "/message"))

If deemed helpful, would it need a check if there's a line number present in the Augeas error? Or does it always come with a line number? (I'm not familiar with Augeas...)

(Untested code by the way :stuck_out_tongue:)

issue_title: Temporarily ignore cryptography deprecation warnings in verify_renewable_cert_sig
issue_body:
At PyCon, one of the cryptography/pyopenssl maintainers switched some code around in  `certbot.crypto_util.verify_renewable_cert_sig` to use cryptography rather than pyopenssl to try and work around some segfaults on some obscure systems. He told me at PyCon the methods he was using because of our need to support ancient versions were triggering deprecation warnings, but we didn't need to worry about them ripping it out anytime soon.

I didn't think anything of it because deprecation warnings aren't usually shown to users, but cryptography use their custom warning type which are shown. We haven't been seeing this because they don't show up in our pinned versions, but you can see it by upgrading cryptography in the venv and running `certbot certificates`.

We should use the `warnings` module to ignore these warnings during the duration of the method.

issue_body:
Certbot's built in log rotation prevents logs from being handled in the manner defined by the system administrator. I see that in #3382 ([2bdf125](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/commit/2bdf1258ad9db1a3a0681a3b81614c93a1d5b96d)) we discovered one aspect of this issue and addressed that by increasing the number of logs kept by Certbot to 1000.

I object to this fix for a few reasons:
1) Systems not specifically configured to address Certbot's logs will end up with 1001 log files.
2) It is not typically in the utility's scope to address log rotation.
3) An administrator seeking to control Certbot's logging must now consider how to work around the existing log rotation, instead of simply addressing log size & count in the normal fashion.

For these reasons, please consider removing the built in (and hard coded) log rotation. That will bring Certbot's log file handling into line with other *nix utilities. If it remains necessary to call logger.handlers.RotatingFileHandler; please then set the size & count to 0; this will cause it to constantly write to the same file. 

Another note is that some/all log rotators can inform a process when a log is rotated; it may be desirable to enable this integration. Example: `path_to_pid_cmd_file` section in the [newsyslog.conf man page](http://www.unix.com/man-page/freebsd/5/newsyslog.conf/) & `postrotate` section in the [logrotate man page](https://linux.die.net/man/8/logrotate).

Lastly, adding a section to the documentation on Certbot's logging may be advised.

Thank you!

The cause of #4044 is that although `setUpClass` wasn't added until Python 2.7, [nose manually runs this method for us](https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/blob/master/nose/suite.py#L308). We should make sure our tests catch problems like this in the future.

1. Stop using `nose` and rely either on standard Python scripts for running tests or use another program to run our tests.

issue_title: Simple lookahead strategy for gas and raiden transactions
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

As discussed in the Raiden workshop we need a simple strategy to handle the user running out of gas while running raiden. For a more complete solution look at [this](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1861) issue.

## Task

- [ ] Calculate/refer to the gas tables for the gas costs of all channel operations and add them to [constants.py](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/4e0bfe55daea6f65ce439bc7a359b629dc0ae7cb/raiden/constants.py).

- [ ] When user joins a token network calculate the gas required for the opening of all channels and the gas they would require for closing/setting them and if he does not have enough ETH to cover for it refuse to join the token network and provide a big warning message.

- [ ] When the user opens a channel do the same as above but for only that one channel.

- [ ] If the user opens Raiden and we don't have enough funds for an entire channel cycle warn the user though the CLI.

issue_title: Use obj_or_id more widely to make APIs more flexible
issue_body:
`canvasapi.util.obj_or_id` makes it easier to implement flexible methods that accept either an `int` ID or an object that has an `id` attribute. This could be used more widely than it is right now. Just to give a few examples:

- `Canvas.get_course_nickname(self, course_id)` could accept a `Course` object for `course_id`.

- `Section.get_submission(self, assignment_id, user_id, **kwargs)` could accept an `Assignment` object for `assignment_id`, and could accept a `User` object for `user_id`.

- `Course.conversations_batch_update(self, conversation_ids, event)` could accept a list of `Conversation` objects for `conversation_ids`. Note that allowing this would require applying `obj_or_id` to all values in a list rather than merely a single value.

issue_title: If a task kills a worker, an error should be pushed to the relevant driver.
issue_body:
Consider the following task which kills a driver.

```python
import ray

ray.init(redirect_output=True)

@ray.remote
def f():
  eval("exit()")

ray.get(f.remote())
```

This script will hang and no error message will be pushed to the driver. At the very least, an error message should be pushed to the driver. We may also want to prevent the driver from hanging. This can be done in two ways.

1. Figure out the object IDs that were supposed to be created by the task and put an object in the object store.
2. Figure out the object IDs that were supposed to be created by the task and put a value in Redis indicating that those objects failed. Then in ray.get, periodically check Redis to see if that value is present.

issue_title: Single object transfer does not fully utilize network bandwidth.
issue_body:
On two machines `m4.16xlarge` (in the same placement group), I see the following.

Benchmarking network bandwidth using iperf consistently gives 11.1Gbit/s. E.g.,

On machine 1

```
iperf -s
```

On machine 2

```
iperf -c <machine-1-ip>
```

The latter prints

```
TCP window size:  325 KByte (default)
------------------------------------------------------------
[  3] local 172.31.19.140 port 58170 connected with 172.31.31.94 port 5001
[ ID] Interval       Transfer     Bandwidth
[  3]  0.0-10.0 sec  12.9 GBytes  11.1 Gbits/sec
```

On the other hand, transferring a large object with Ray gets about half of that. To see, start Ray on two machines (with one custom resource on the remote machine).

On machine 1

```
ray start --head --redis-port=6379
```

On machine 2

```
ray start --redis-address=<head-node-ip>:6379 --resources='{"CustomResource": 1}'
```

Then run the following on machine 1

```python
import numpy as np
import ray
ray.init(redis_address="<machine-1-ip>:6379")

# This task will be scheduled on machine 2.
@ray.remote(resources={"CustomResource": 1})
def f(size):
    return np.ones(size, dtype=np.uint8)

x_id = f.remote(10 ** 8)
ray.wait([x_id])  # Wait for the task to finish executing.
%time x = ray.get(x_id)  # 178ms

x_id = f.remote(10 ** 9)
ray.wait([x_id])  # Wait for the task to finish executing.
%time x = ray.get(x_id)  # 1.71s
```

In both cases, we're getting roughly **4.5Gbit/s**.

There are some points we should talk about in `get_grid`:

- Should `x_name` and `y_name` be inputs? If those are changed, any components in CliMT that use lat or lon dimensions will break (unexpectedly). I feel like if the user wants to change those for diagnostic reasons, they should do it at the time that they output a netCDF file.
- What is the best way to supply top and bottom pressure to `get_grid`? The two candidates seem to be setting constants in the constants library, or kwargs. I feel like setting constants is a convoluted way to go about this. There's no reason why the surface pressure should be the reference pressure. And I don't currently understand why model top pressure must be read by a component as a Sympl constant - can't the component just read `air_pressure_on_interface_levels` to determine model top pressure? That way would ensure we don't unintentionally develop in consistencies between the model top pressure in the state and the Sympl constant.

@JoyMonteiro also brought up a point about e.g. ground schemes wanting to be on separate grids from the atmosphere. The way CliMT is currently set up, I would see that being done by `get_grid`, or by splitting `get_grid` into multiple functions (`get_atmosphere_grid`, `get_land_grid`, `get_ocean_grid`, etc.). This also requires couplers. What do you think of this, @JoyMonteiro ?

issue_title: query vector from hnsw index not found in top K
issue_body:
This is more a question than a bug report.

Building a rather large index with hnsw method, > 1M unit vectors (100d) with l2 space, M=40, efConstruction=1000, efSearch in 100-1000 range, default params otherwise (using v1.7.2)
For every vector in index, querying for top 1000 nearest neighbors. This works like a charm and the results are very accurate (and fast!) and because the vectors being queried are also used to create the index, the closest vector is always itself. However, there are very few cases ( < 100 ) where this isn't the case, i.e. the query vector is not in the top 1000 and the results are more or less relevant. 

My question would be, is this simply a consequence of the approximate nature of the method ? Something that has to do with how the heuristics work when building the index and all ?

Thanks in advance !

Please, use fast crc32 from zlib. It is about 18 times faster:

import zlib
import couchbase.crc32 as couch_slow_crc
import time

def Func1(amax=100000):
   t1=time.clock()
   for i in range(amax):
      x=couch_slow_crc.crc32_hash(`i`)
   t2=time.clock()
   return t2-t1
   
def Func2(amax=100000):
   t1=time.clock()
   for i in range(amax):
      x=(zlib.crc32(`i`)>>16)&0x7fff
   t2=time.clock()
   return t2-t1


def Func3(amax=100000):
   for i in range(amax):
      v1=(zlib.crc32(`i`)>>16)&0x7fff
      v2=couch_slow_crc.crc32_hash(`i`)
      if v1!=v2:
         print 'conflict %d %x %x'%(i,v1,v2)
   t2=time.clock()

t1=Func1()
t2=Func2()
print 'time couch',t1
print 'time zlib',t2
print 'speed',t1/t2
Func3()

#######################################
time couch 1.83402449707
time zlib 0.0990188503142
speed 18.5219732531
